data.title: FTC Release: FTC Issues Biennial Report to Congress on the National Do Not Call Registry
data.description:
In FY 2025, the agency received more than 2.6 million complaints and 4.8 million new numbers were added to the DNC Registry

The Federal Trade Commission issued its biennial report to Congress on the National Do Not Call (DNC) Registry that shows consumers placed more than 258 million telephone numbers on the Registry as of the end of fiscal year 2025, an increase of more than 4.8 million from the previous fiscal year.

View Press Release: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/01/ftc-issues-biennial-report-congress-national-do-not-call-registry

data.title: Reddit jumps on implementing fix for platform-wide errors
data.description:
** Shares of social media platform Reddit rise 6.1% to $259.01 in afternoon trading

** Co says issue with elevated errors across Reddit.com and native apps has been identified and a fix is being implemented

** Earlier, Downdetector showed Reddit down for 77,279 users in U.S. as of 11:46 a.m. ET

** RDDT stock rose nearly 40% in 2025

data.description:
RNS Number : 7720O Premier Miton Group PLC 13 January 2026  

13 January 2026

Premier Miton Group plc

('Premier Miton' or the 'Company')

PDMR/Director Dealing

Premier Miton Group plc (AIM: PMI) has been notified that yesterday, awards over a total of 81,154 ordinary shares in the Company ('Shares') in the form of a nil cost contingent share awards, granted in accordance with the 2016 LTIP rules on 1 June 2022 for the performance period 1 October 2022 to 30 September 2025, which vested on 13 January 2026, were automatically exercised via irrevocable market instructions placed by the Employee Benefit Trusts ('EBTs') on 13 January 2026, in favour of Directors Mike O'Shea and Piers Harrison. The Shares are satisfied from Employee Benefit Trusts.

Of those Shares, 16,943 Shares were sold by Piers Harrison to satisfy the resultant income tax and National Insurance liabilities.  The balances of 47,340 Shares and 16,871 Shares will transfer to Mike O'Shea and Piers Harrison respectively, in satisfaction of their awards.

Mike O'Shea's and Piers Harrison's resultant shareholdings in the Company are 4,549,508 Shares1,2 (representing approximately 2.79% of the issued share capital) and 370,2301 Shares (representing approximately 0.23% of the issued share capital) respectively.

data.title: Even Oil Companies Are Nervous About Trump's Crackdown on Offshore Wind — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Avi Salzman

President Donald Trump has made no secret of his disdain for wind power. He even brought it up at a meeting with oil executives on Friday that was ostensibly about drilling in Venezuela.

"By the way, in case you people don't know, I'm not much of a windmill person," Trump said. "My goal is to not let any windmill be built."

His administration is making good on that promise, forcing work to stop on all offshore wind projects on Dec. 22 because of what officials said was a national security concern.

The Department of the Interior said that the turbines may block radar signals, based on classified reports. On Monday, a judge lifted the stop work order for one project off Rhode Island, but the other four that were under construction remain stalled, losing millions of dollars each day. Each project had already gone through a government permitting process lasting more than five years, which included reviews by defense and security officials.

On its face, Trump's blockage of wind projects looks like a good thing for fossil fuel producers. Wind power competes against resources like coal and natural gas, all of which are used to generate electricity. But oil companies also rely on predictable permitting decisions. And some have already raised concerns that their fossil fuel investments could be derailed by a change in presidential administrations.

"I certainly would like to see those projects that have been permitted in the past continue to be developed," Colette Hirstius, president of Shell USA, told the Financial Times last year. Shell previously walked away from its U.S. offshore wind investments, but sees the permitting uncertainty as a threat to its oil projects too, including in the Gulf of Mexico. "That type of permitting uncertainty has been utilized to undermine the permits that we have in the past," she said.

The American Petroleum Institute, or API, which advocates for the oil and gas industry, has made permitting reform its number one goal this year. "It takes far too long to build the infrastructure required to move energy where it's needed," API President Mike Sommers told reporters. "Red tape, delay, endless litigation, all drive up costs, limit supply, and strain reliability."

But permitting reform is now stalled because of offshore wind. Congress appeared to be closer than ever to passing a bipartisan bill that could speed the permitting process both for fossil fuel projects and renewables.

The bill was also expected to include provisions making it harder for administrations to cancel permits that had already been issued. That wouldn't just help offshore wind — it could also have stopped President Joe Biden from canceling the Keystone XL pipeline project for natural gas during his administration.

But Democrats walked away from the bill last month because of the Trump administration's moratorium on wind projects. They do not want to move forward if the administration is going to unilaterally cancel projects it doesn't like.

Asked by a Barron's reporter on Tuesday about whether API would back offshore wind in order to get a permitting deal, Sommers said his group is working with trade groups for alternative energy.

"It's time for both sides to put their weapons down," he said. "We're going to need... a lot more energy going forward, and that means all sources of energy. We are not in competition with each other."

The oil and gas industry will never fully embrace renewable energy. But to grow faster, it may need to quietly start advocating for those much-maligned windmills.

Laura Sanicola contributed reporting.

Write to Avi Salzman at avi.salzman@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: European Stocks Close Mixed After Cautious Session
data.description:
BRUSSELS/FRANKFURT/PARIS (dpa-AFX) - European stocks closed mixed on Tuesday as concerns about geopolitical tensions and U.S. President Donald Trump's new tariff threat rendered the mood cautious, forcing traders to refrain from making big moves. Some corporate updates provided a bit of direction.

The latest inflation data from the U.S. came in line with expectations, but raised expectations of more monetary easing in the coming months.

The pan European Stoxx 600 edged down marginally. The U.K.'s FTSE 100 slipped 0.03%, Germany's DAX edged up 0.06%, and France's CAC 40 closed down by 0.14%. Switzerland's SMI ended down by 0.46%.

Among other markets in Europe, Belgium, Denmark, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal and Turkiye closed higher.

Greece, Iceland, Ireland, Poland and Russia ended weak, while Czech Republic, Finland, Spain and Sweden closed flat.

In the UK market, Whitbread surged more than 7% as pressure on margins eased after the company said the impact of the UK budget on costs would be lower than previously expected.

Pershing Square Holdings climbed 2.7%. BP, Shell, Melrose Industries and British American Foods gained 2 to 2.7%.

JD Sports Fashion, Rolls-Royce Holdings, Rio Tinto, Fresnillo, Endeavour Mining, Beazley and Polar Capital Technology Trust also ended notably higher.

Smith & Nephew, Kingfisher, Convatec Group, Entain, Rentokil Initial, Vodafone Group, Admiral Group, Severn Trent, Persimmon, ICG, National Grid and 3i Group lost 2 to 4%.

In the German market, Symrise moved up more than 5%. Symrise AG shares surged nearly 7% after the company announced that it is in advanced discussions with potential buyers regarding the divestment of its terpenes business.

Zalando gained about 4.75% after Barclays upgraded the stock from equalweight to overweight and raised its price target to EUR 35.00, an increas of EUR 7.00.

Infineon and MTU Aero Engines both gained about 2%. Siemens gained nearly 1.5%, while Siemens Energy, Fresenius, Rheinmetall, Brenntag, Daimler Truck Holding and Commerzbank also closed on positive note.

Fresenius Medical Care, Heidelberg Materials, Continental, Deutsche Telekom, BMW and Deutsche Boerse lost 2 to 3%.

Hannover Rueck, Bayer, Merck, E.ON, Adidas, Qiagen and Munich RE also ended notably lower.

In the French market, TotalEnergies, Safran, Airbus, Capgemini, Eurofins Scientific, Pernod Ricard and STMicroElectronics gained 1 to 2.3%.

Vinci, TP, Saint Gobain, Stellantis, Veolia Environment, Carrefour, Edenred, Sanofi and Thales lost 2 to 4.3%.

AXA, Kering, Engie, Bouygues, Unibail Rodamco, Dassault Systemes and Publicis Groupe also closed weak.

In economic news, UK retail sales grew at a slower pace in December despite festive season as consumers cut back spending on higher household bills, the British Retail Consortium said Tuesday.

Total retail sales increased 1.2% in December from the previous year compared to an annual growth of 3.2% in the same period last year.

Food sales rose 3.1% on a yearly basis, while non-food sales decreased 0.3%. In-store non-food sales dropped 0.5% and online non-food sales fell 0.1%.

The online penetration rate increased to 38.6% in December from 38.5% in the last year.

Data showed France's central government budget deficit narrowed to EUR 155.4 billion at the end of November 2025, from EUR 172.5 billion in the same month a year earlier.

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Roblox Expected to Give Cautious Forecast for 2026 — Market Talk
data.description: Roblox will likely provide a cautious outlook for 2026, Raymond James analysts say in a note. Investors have concerns about Roblox's prior projection for margins to decline from infrastructure and safety investments, and they worry about whether the company will have more viral hits as it laps the rise of popular games from last year, the analysts say. "We believe the company is incentivized to be cautious," they say, noting even a 20% year-over-year booking growth rate could disappoint investors' expectations. "While we believe a lot of this concern is being reflected in the stock price given its recent tumble, we see this quarter as a potential soft spot as investors come to terms with the 2026 outlook," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Food Preservatives Might Slightly Increase Cancer Risk, Study Suggests
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A new study from France, published in The BMJ, suggested that people who eat more foods containing preservatives may have a slightly higher risk of developing cancer.

To study possible long-term effects of food preservatives, the researchers examined diet and health data collected between 2009 and 2023. The data included 105,260 people aged 15 and above, with an average age of 42. Notably, none of the participants had cancer at the start of the study.

During the study period, participants regularly recorded what they ate using detailed 24-hour food diaries over an average period of 7.5 years. Meanwhile, cancer cases were tracked using health surveys and official medical and death records until December 31, 2023.

On the other side, the researchers analyzed 17 different preservatives, including citric acid, lecithins, sulfites, sodium nitrite, potassium sorbate, and potassium nitrate, which were grouped into two types: non-antioxidants, which prevent the growth of microbes or slow food spoilage, and antioxidants, which help stop food from deteriorating by limiting oxygen exposure.

At the end of the study, 4,226 participants were diagnosed with cancer, which included 1,208 cases of breast cancer, 508 prostate cancers, 352 colorectal cancers, and 2,158 other types.

However, the researchers stressed that since it is an observational study, it does not prove that preservatives cause cancer. Still, they believe that the findings could be used as evidence in the ongoing debate about the health impact of food preservatives.

'This study brings new insights for the future re-evaluation of the safety of these food additives by health agencies, considering the balance between benefit and risk for food preservation and cancer,' the authors concluded.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Meta's Nuclear Data Center Deals May Require Over $14 Billion Investment
data.description:
BloombergNEF analysts have outlined the scale of investment that could be required for Meta Platforms to secure nuclear power for its expanding data center footprint, estimating that more than $14 billion may need to be committed to next-generation reactors developed by Oklo Inc. and privately held TerraPower. The research suggests these projects could deliver around 690 megawatts of fission capacity from TerraPower and roughly 1.2 gigawatts from Oklo over the next decade, following a series of nuclear agreements Meta announced last week. At this stage, neither Oklo nor TerraPower has regulatory approval for a commercial system, and neither has yet demonstrated the ability to produce power at full commercial scale, underscoring the early-stage nature of the commitments.

BloombergNEF framed the projected spending as a reflection of the technology sector's growing interest in nuclear energy as US electricity demand rises and data center operators look for reliable, around-the-clock supply. While advanced reactors are positioned as a source of carbon-free power, analysts cautioned that early deployments are expected to be costly and time-consuming. Chris Gadomski, BloombergNEF's lead nuclear analyst, noted that initial cost estimates and construction timelines in North America and Europe have historically come in well below actual outcomes. Within the Meta agreement, Oklo is developing a 75-megawatt reactor design and planning a 1.2-gigawatt campus in Ohio, which Gadomski estimated could require 16 reactors at roughly $350 million to $400 million each, with initial systems potentially delivering power by 2030.

TerraPower's arrangement with Meta is structured around two 345-megawatt reactors, with options for up to six additional units, and BloombergNEF estimated the first reactors could cost about $4 billion apiece before declining as experience builds. TerraPower has said it expects costs to fall further over time, targeting an eventual price closer to $1 billion per unit. Alongside these advanced projects, Meta has also agreed to purchase power from three conventional nuclear reactors operated by Vistra Corp. in Ohio and Pennsylvania, a set of deals that researchers at ClearView Energy Partners said positions Meta among a small group of technology companies with the financial capacity and risk tolerance to support emerging nuclear designs.

data.title: 'These Divided Isles' Review: A History of Troubles
data.description:
By Crawford Gribben

The more things change, the more they stay the same: That could have been the subtitle of Philip Stephens's vivid and provocative book on the tangled relationship between Britain and Ireland. "These Divided Isle s" describes how terrorists and visionaries followed a wandering path to peace at the end of the 20th century, and the reasons why the fragile equilibrium has been put in jeopardy again.

Mr. Stephens, a longtime journalist at the Financial Times, puts his own story at the center of this book. Although he grew up in London, he spent childhood holidays in the 1950s and '60s with his mother's family in Ireland's County Mayo. That may be where Mr. Stephens learned the history that sits uneasily in his prologue — a sequence of generalizations that pits "the Irish" against "the British," but which effectively shows how, for many centuries, the kingdom of Ireland was managed as an English colony.

When the narrative moves into the 20th century, however, Mr. Stephens comes into his own. He provides sharp observations on the Easter Rising of 1916, the war for independence and the civil war of the early 1920s. He follows the establishment of the Catholic Church's vast influence over law, politics and culture in the south, while the new nation's protestant community almost disappeared through assimilation, migration and sometimes persecution.

In Northern Ireland — those counties that remained part of the U.K. after the rest of the island was granted independence in 1922 — a "protestant parliament" governed a "protestant state." Catholics there were unhappy with their fate, even if, in the postwar period, they enjoyed a better standard of living than their coreligionists in the south. But inequality is relative, and when civil-rights campaigns took off in the 1960s, northern Catholics had plenty of complaints. Unionists, who wished to preserve Northern Ireland's place in the U.K., were largely unsympathetic. When the government in Belfast dismissed demands for progressive change, and political outsiders resorted to physical force, the northern "troubles" began.

By the late 1960s, Northern Ireland had drifted into another civil war. Republicans fought to destabilize the U.K's control over the North while, as Mr. Stephens notes, politicians on both sides of the border were accused of providing them material support. Republicans and the British government began to negotiate in the 1970s, and Mr. Stephens admires the leaders who worked for peace, especially because "moderates on both sides feared extremists on their own side more than they trusted moderates on the other side." As a journalist, Mr. Stephens reported on the slow, painful birth of the 1998 peace accord known as the Good Friday Agreement. And he watched as other politicians, less invested in the negotiations, breathed a sigh of relief and turned their attention elsewhere.

Yet, less than two decades later, the province was again at the center of political debate. Before the 2016 Brexit referendum, both Ireland and the U.K. were part of the European Union; in that year, Britain's prime minister, David Cameron, determined to put an end to decades of squabbling on the Tory right about whether it had been right to join. He asked voters to decide whether the U.K. should remain part of the EU. A majority of Northern Ireland's votes indicated a desire to remain. But across the U.K., the result was Brexit.

Mr. Cameron resigned, and Theresa May, who had campaigned against Brexit, took up the task of making it work. The EU played hardball, using Ireland as the means, rather than the end, of negotiation. The Good Friday Agreement had made the Irish border as easy to cross as the border between England and Scotland — and the British government wanted it to stay that way. But the U.K.'s border with Ireland was also its border with the EU — and that couldn't remain invisible under Brexit.

Boris Johnson, campaigning for the Conservative leadership in 2019, promised Northern Ireland's Unionists that he would preserve the U.K.'s borders. Yet, once in power, his government submitted to the demand of European negotiators for a customs border between Britain and Northern Ireland. The priority, negotiators finally agreed, was to protect the borders of the EU — and the Good Friday Agreement's frictionless border between the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland — by dismantling the territorial integrity of the U.K. The absurd result means that goods shipping from Liverpool to Belfast, both represented in the U.K. parliament, may be subject to EU customs checks.

Meanwhile, in Northern Ireland, politics were shifting with demographics. Census results in 2021 showed that Catholics now outnumber protestants. Local elections in 2022 saw the pro-unification party Sinn Féin become the largest party in Northern Ireland. Mr. Stephens's conclusion is that Unionism "was the loser from Brexit." Unionists, who dominated Northern Ireland for most of its history, are now a minority.

That leaves many Irish republicans in an unexpected position. As Mr. Stephens reports, opinion polls show that two-thirds of southern voters would support an end to partition, but they wouldn't extend meaningful accommodation to the Unionists who would find themselves in the Republic of Ireland. Many northern Catholics say they would prefer to stick with the sometimes contradictory status quo. And the end of partition would be costly: While the Republic of Ireland is now one of the wealthiest economies in Western Europe, Mr. Stephens argues, Northern Ireland has become one of its poorest regions. Reunification would precipitate a lasting financial shock.

Mr. Stephens pulls back from drawing the obvious conclusion about Northern Ireland's present constitutional status. After Brexit, the province is notionally governed by Westminster even as it remains subject to customs regulations made in a European parliament in which it is not represented. As Mr. Stephens reminds us, there is no "escape route from history." After centuries of struggle for self-determination, this part of Ireland is once again a colony.

  • Mr. Gribben is a professor of history at Queen's University Belfast.

data.title: Measles cases in South Carolina rise by 124 to 434, state health department says
data.description: The South Carolina health department reported 434 measles cases related to the ongoing outbreak in the state on Tuesday, 124 additional cases since its last update on Friday.

data.title: Quantum Promises AI-Level Disruption. Don't Panic Just Yet. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

How do you prepare for a future that's already here?

Interest in quantum computing, which has the potential to be the next disruptive technology, reached a fever pitch last year, punctuated by headlines about developments in error correction and scaling. Still, its impact on traditional compute remains to be seen, UBS analysts wrote on Tuesday.

"In the same way that AI has created concerns of disruption risk to many incumbents in software, we believe quantum will do the same," the firm said. But this risk is difficult to quantify. Analysts presented several possible scenarios for adoption, including a world where the technology falls short of investors' lofty expectations.

In one scenario, quantum is embraced across the pharmaceutical and financial industries, two areas where it is said to show promise, and tapped to expedite the training of artificial-intelligence models. Ultra-powerful quantum processors will do much of the heavy lifting in hybrid systems, which combine classical and quantum computing. This, in turn, could hurt classical compute demand.

Alternatively, enterprises may rush to adopt less sophisticated quantum systems for fear of losing their competitive edge, with a focus on hybrid systems where classical computers do most of the work. UBS expects this scenario to have an aggregate positive impact on traditional compute.

Then there's the possibility that quantum computers don't catch on, as their capabilities haven't advanced enough to justify large-scale spending on the technology. Similarly, enterprises may be reluctant to adopt quantum given their existing investments in traditional compute.

It may be too early to tell which approach to quantum computing is most effective, but UBS is betting on the superconducting and trapped-ion systems developed by International Business Machines and Alphabet as well as smaller pure plays Rigetti Computing, IonQ, and Honeywell-owned Quantinuum.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Arm Stock Is Falling. Its Biggest Customer Might Be Masking Larger Problems. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Earnings from Arm Holdings have consistently met or exceeded Wall Street's expectations in recent quarters. However, that outperformance belies the chip designer's reliance on its flagship investor, analysts at BofA Securities say.

Japan's SoftBank Group owns around 90% of Arm, and it is also the company's cornerstone customer. Once SoftBank's purchases are taken out of the equation, Arm's growth prospects look dim heading into 2026, BofA said.

The firm downgraded Arm stock to Neutral from Buy and lowered its price target to $120 from $145 in a research note.

U.S.-listed depositary receipts of Arm slipped by 1.6% to $109.39 on Tuesday. Shares are down 22% over the past 12 months as of Monday's close despite a series of solid earnings reports last year.

SoftBank accounted for 35% of Arm's design licensing business in the September quarter — a number that has grown throughout the past year. Under other circumstances, SoftBank may be a "legitimate independent customer," BofA said, but the investment company's enormous ownership stake in Arm raises concerns about circular financing and conflicts of interest. It also obscures Arm's financial results.

"Notably, [SoftBank's] contribution continues to outgrow management's original expectation, or put it another way, continues to help ARM meet guided numbers which would have otherwise missed by tens of [millions of dollars]," wrote BofA analysts led by Vivek Arya.

Barron's has reached out to Arm and SoftBank for comment.

The growing reliance on SoftBank comes as Arm enters a challenging year for its overall business. BofA remains optimistic about Arm's long-term potential as a vendor for data-center servers. But consumer products like smartphones still comprise the vast majority of Arm's royalties, and near-term demand in these markets is under pressure, the firm said.

Global smartphone shipments, in particular, could decline in 2026 due to increased memory costs and supply constraints, BofA predicts. The firm isn't alone in that assessment: Last week, analysts at Mizuho forecast the global market for handsets to be flat to down 2% in 2026 after rising about 2% in 2025.

BofA expects Arm's royalty and licensing revenue to decelerate into 2026, with licensing growth slowing to single digits in 2027 and 2028 from an estimated 32% in 2025.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: KYMR: KT-621 oral degrader advances in AD and asthma, supported by strong data and strategic partnerships
data.description:
The conference highlighted progress in developing first-in-class oral degraders for immunological diseases, with KT-621 showing robust efficacy and safety in early trials and advancing into Phase 2b studies for AD and asthma. Strategic partnerships and a strong cash position support a broad pipeline and growth into 2029.

Original document: Kymera Therapeutics, Inc. [KYMR] Slides Release — Jan. 13 2026

data.title: Lynas CEO Amanda Lacaze to Step Down After 12-Year Turnaround
data.description:
Lynas Rare Earths Ltd (LYSDY) said Chief Executive Officer Amanda Lacaze will step down at the end of June, closing a 12-year period in which she helped build the company into one of the most significant challengers to China's dominance in rare earths. The miner, now valued at around $10 billion, has played a central role in efforts by Japan and other countries to reduce reliance on Chinese supply, at a time when critical minerals have become increasingly entangled with geopolitical and trade considerations.

The leadership change comes as Lynas shares rose as much as 4.1%, tracking broader gains across rare-earth producers following a Group of Seven meeting on Monday focused on addressing vulnerabilities in critical mineral supply chains. China has previously used its dominance in rare-earth processing as leverage in trade discussions with the US and in negotiations with other countries, a backdrop that continues to shape investor interest in producers operating outside Beijing's orbit.

Lacaze, one of a small number of female chief executives in the mining industry, took over Lynas when it was close to collapse in 2014 and oversaw a significant turnaround. During her tenure, the company's market value increased from about A$400 million to close to A$15 billion, according to Chairman John Humphrey. Lynas now operates mines in Australia and processing facilities in Malaysia, where it began producing heavy rare earths last year and is expanding capacity to meet demand from applications such as electric vehicle motors, wind turbines, drones, and consumer electronics.

data.title: ACHR: eVTOL aircraft set for public flights by 2026, with LA Olympics as a major showcase
data.description:
The company is advancing eVTOL aircraft for urban and defense use, targeting certification between 2026 and the 2028 LA Olympics. Key milestones include public demonstration flights, a major Olympic partnership, and a conservative approach to design and manufacturing. Strong capital reserves support commercialization and selective acquisitions.
